summary: Macedonian king (356-323 BC), son of Philip II and pupil of Aristotle, who conquered the Achaemenid Persian Empire of Darius III at the battles of the Granicus, Issus, and Gaugamela, besieged Tyre, campaigned into India as far as the Hydaspes River, and died in Babylon at age 32. His empire fractured among his generals (the Diadochi) in the Wars of the Diadochi, and his exploits generated an enormous medieval legendary tradition (the Alexander Romance) across Christian, Islamic, Persian, and African sources, in which he is frequently identified with the horned, wall-building Quranic figure Dhu'l-Qarnayn.

works.name: General / Giveaway Identifiers
works.description: How Alexander is identified in giveaway lines: the Macedonian king, son of Philip II, who conquered the Achaemenid Persian Empire and famously cut through the Gordian Knot at Gordium. Clues also reference his horse Bucephalus, his elite Companion cavalry, and his adoption of Persian customs after his conquests.

clues.clue: Conquered the Achaemenid Persian Empire; legendarily cut through the Gordian Knot at Gordium, fulfilling a prophecy that its undoer would rule Asia
examples: name this Macedonian ruler who conquered the Achaemenid Empire.
the Gordian Knot was severed by what Macedonian conqueror of the Achaemenid Empire?

clues.clue: Rode the horse Bucephalus and reportedly gave elaborate funerals to it and his dog Peritas
examples: depicts the elaborate funerals this king ordered for his beloved dog Peritas and horse Bucephalus
tutored by Aristotle and owned the horse Bucephalus, was supposedly buried in a honey-filled sarcophagus

clues.clue: Led an elite shock cavalry force called the Companions, armed with the xyston lance, alongside infantry phalangites and hypaspists
examples: This elite military force created by Philip II was typically personally led by Alexander during his battles. This early shock cavalry force was typically armed with the xyston.
what king gave rousing speeches to his phalangites and Companion cavalry before battles at Issus and Gaugamela?

clues.clue: After conquering Persia, adopted foreign customs, including requiring subjects to kneel and kiss his feet (proskynesis)
examples: name this Greek ruler who adopted many foreign customs after conquering Persia for Macedon.
A treasonous scribe enticed two pages to assassinate this ruler out of anger at his adoption of the custom of making subjects kneel and kiss his feet, called proskynesis.

clues.clue: At a mass wedding at Susa, married off many of his generals to Persian noblewomen and paid off 20,000 talents of his soldiers' debts
examples: Up to 20,000 talents were distributed to pay debts during an event in which this ruler married off many of his generals to foreign wives at Susa.

clues.clue: Married the Bactrian princess Roxane after conquering Bactria
examples: Later in life, this ruler married the princess Roxana after conquering Bactria.

works.name: Tutelage under Aristotle & Youth
works.description: Alexander was tutored by Aristotle, who agreed to teach him on the condition that Aristotle's hometown Stagira be rebuilt; the two mainly met at Mieza, alongside future Diadochi like Cassander and Ptolemy. Alexander's birth was itself framed in legend, and his old tutor was later implicated (apocryphally) in his death.

clues.clue: Aristotle agreed to tutor him on condition that his hometown Stagira be rebuilt; they mainly interacted at Mieza, where Aristotle also taught future Diadochi Cassander and Ptolemy I
examples: The relationship between these two men began on the condition that the city of Stagira be rebuilt.
These two mostly interacted at Mieza where the older of these two men also taught the future Diadochi Cassander and Ptolemy I.

clues.clue: As a student of Aristotle, allegedly told the philosopher Diogenes of Sinope to move out of his sunlight; later, as a military leader, sent donations back to Aristotle's school, the Lyceum
examples: Diogenes of Sinope once asked that famous man of this name to move [out of his sunlight]
A military leader with this name sent donations to his teacher's school, the Lyceum.

clues.clue: An apocryphal conspiracy theory blamed his death on poison supplied by his old tutor Aristotle, delivered via the regent Cassander
examples: Apocryphal sources blame it on Cassander, who used poison provided by Aristotle.

clues.clue: The historian Callisthenes, a great-nephew of Aristotle who accompanied Alexander's campaign, was killed for conspiring against him with the page Hermolaus
examples: A scholar in the employ of one of these men, who was also the great-nephew of the other, was killed for conspiring to kill that employer with the page Hermolaus.

clues.clue: Was reportedly born after his mother Olympias's womb was struck by a lightning bolt from Zeus, and a temple of Artemis at Ephesus supposedly burned down the same day
examples: This ruler was supposedly born after the womb of his mother Olympias was struck by a lightning bolt from Zeus.
Plutarch claims that since Artemis was attending to this ruler's birth, her temple at Ephesus burnt down on the same day.

works.name: Battle of the Granicus
works.description: Alexander's first major battle against the Persian Empire (334 BC), fought against local satraps commanded by the Greek mercenary general Memnon of Rhodes. His officer Cleitus the Black famously saved his life at this battle, an act Alexander later repaid with Cleitus's death.

clues.clue: Fought his first major battle against Persian satraps commanded by the Greek mercenary general Memnon of Rhodes
examples: In the 333 BCE Battle of Issus, this Macedonian conquered Darius III after destroying the satraps led by Memnon of Rhodes at the earlier Battle of Granicus.

clues.clue: His officer Cleitus the Black saved his life at this battle by severing the arm of the Persian noble Spithridates; years later, Alexander killed Cleitus in a drunken quarrel
examples: Give this name of two generals nicknamed 'the White' and 'the Black.' The latter allegedly saved his king from death by severing the arm of Spithridates; he was repaid with death in a drunken fight.
The legitimacy of this ruler was questioned by a man who died after this leader impaled him with a javelin, although earlier this ruler's life had been saved by that man at the Battle of Granicus.

works.name: Siege of Tyre
works.description: Alexander besieged the island city of Tyre (modern-day Lebanon) in 332 BC, building a massive causeway that permanently transformed the island into a peninsula. The siege followed the Battle of Issus and preceded the Siege of Gaza on his march toward Egypt.

clues.clue: Besieged the island city of Tyre by building a massive causeway (mole) that permanently transformed it into a peninsula
examples: A siege led by this man was carried out through the construction of a causeway that permanently transformed an island city in modern-day Lebanon into a peninsula.
His other exploits included building a causeway to sack Tyre.

clues.clue: Before the siege, dreamed of a satyr, which his seers interpreted as 'sa Tyros' ('Tyre is yours')
examples: According to Plutarch, this man dreamed of a satyr during a siege, which his seers deemed meant 'sa Tyros', or 'Tyre is yours'.

clues.clue: This siege followed the Battle of Issus and preceded the Siege of Gaza; the besiegers broke through Tyre's legendary high walls after a six-month siege
examples: Name this siege of a Phoenician city after the Battle of Issus but before the Siege of Gaza. After half a year, the besiegers finally broke through its legendary high walls.

works.name: Battle of Issus
works.description: Alexander defeated Darius III at Issus (333 BC) near the Pinarus River, after marching through the Pass of Jonah while Darius executed wounded Macedonians left behind at a nearby city. Darius fled the field, abandoning his family, whom Alexander later treated with clemency.

clues.clue: Defeated Darius III at Issus (333 BC) near the Pinarus River, after marching through the Pass of Jonah while Darius executed wounded Macedonians left behind at a nearby city
examples: These two rulers met in battle after one traversed the Pass of Jonah and the other executed wounded men left behind at a city on the Pinarus River.

clues.clue: At Issus, Darius III lost his nerve and fled the battlefield after Alexander led a direct cavalry charge with the Companions
examples: One of these rulers lost courage and fled in two battles after the other led a direct attack with the Companions.

clues.clue: Following the victories at Issus and Gaugamela, the Persian capital Persepolis was burned
examples: The city of Persepolis was burned after these two rulers fought the decisive battles of Issus and Gaugamela.

clues.clue: Used the Persian Royal Road (which passed through the Cilician Gates) to rapidly mobilize his troops during the invasion of Persia
examples: While travelling along the Royal Road, this general passed through the infamous Cilician Gates. This Macedonian general used the Royal Road to quickly mobilize troops during his invasion of Persia.

works.name: Battle of Gaugamela
works.description: Alexander's decisive final battle against Darius III (331 BC), which effectively ended the Achaemenid Persian Empire. Darius fled again and was later killed by his own general Bessus.

clues.clue: At Gaugamela, javelin-throwing Agrianian skirmishers disrupted Darius III's scythed chariots after the battlefield had been cleared of vegetation for the chariots' charge
examples: During a battle between these two rulers, javelin throwing Agriane skirmishers disrupted a charge of scythed chariots that had been prepared by clearing the battlefield of vegetation.

clues.clue: Won this decisive final battle against Darius III, effectively ending the Achaemenid Empire; the rebellious satrap Bessus then killed Darius and was executed a year later at Ecbatana
examples: The rebellious Bessus killed one of these rulers and spent a year resisting the other before his execution in Ecbatana.

works.name: Claim to Divinity & the Oracle of Siwa
works.description: Alexander traveled to the Oracle of Zeus-Ammon at the Siwa Oasis in Egypt, where he was told he was the son of the god Ammon (identified with Zeus). His claimed divine parentage was supported by stories about his mother Olympias's dreams and was later contested by his own philosophers.

clues.clue: Traveled to the Oracle of Zeus-Ammon at the Siwa Oasis in Egypt, where he was told he was the son of the god Ammon, an episode recounted in Arrian's Anabasis
examples: This ruler traveled to the Temple of Zeus-Ammon in Siwa in order to prove the divinity of his father, as recounted in the Anabasis written about his life by the Nicomedian historian Arrian.
This Greek ruler was told at Siwa Oasis that he was actually the son of Amun.

clues.clue: His mother Olympias reportedly recounted dreams of a lightning bolt striking her and of a snake in her bed, used to support belief in his divine parentage
examples: Plutarch reports that stories about a woman dreaming of a lightning bolt striking her body and that woman's husband seeing her in bed with a snake were used to support the belief in this status.

clues.clue: The historian/philosopher Anaxarchus argued for his divinity in a debate; another philosopher who opposed the idea quipped he would 'go away one kiss the poorer'
examples: A historian quipped 'I shall go away one kiss the poorer' after angering his patron by arguing against this status in a debate with Anaxarchus.

clues.clue: Was often depicted with two horns due to conflation with the ram-horned god Amun, an image later linked to the Quranic figure Dhu'l-Qarnayn ('the Two-Horned One')
examples: Because of his divine heritage, Alexander the Great was often depicted with two of these body parts. The title 'Dhu al-Qarnayn' refers to him possessing two of these body parts.

works.name: Indian Campaign & the Battle of the Hydaspes
works.description: Alexander's invasion of the Indian subcontinent (327-325 BC) climaxed at the Battle of the Hydaspes River against King Porus and ended when his exhausted troops mutinied at the Beas River, refusing to advance further.

clues.clue: During the siege of the 'birdless rock' Aornos (at the spur of Pir Sar), attempted to outdo the legendary feat of Hercules by setting up catapults on an eastern summit
examples: That siege during it was at a place known as the 'birdless rock,' Aornos, and was reportedly done in a brave attempt to outdo the mythical Hercules.
In one battle from this campaign, invading forces set up catapults on an eastern summit to besiege the legendary Rock of Aornus.

clues.clue: At the Battle of the Hydaspes River (326 BC, along the modern-day Jhelum River), defeated King Porus by using pikemen to drive Porus's war elephants amok among his own forces; Alexander's horse Bucephalus died from wounds sustained in the battle
examples: In the most famous battle from this campaign, the winning side used pikes to drive war elephants amok among their own forces, leading to the defeat of King Porus.
It basically abruptly ended when troops refused to go past what is now the Beas River; this occurred shortly after a grueling defeat of Porus at a battle that killed the horse Bucephalus.

clues.clue: After defeating Porus, allowed him to continue ruling as a subject king, reportedly because Porus asked to be treated 'as a king would treat another king'; Alexander founded the now-lost city of Nicaea to commemorate the victory
examples: After a battle during this campaign, the losing commander mythically said 'treat me as a king would treat another king,' while the winner founded the now-lost city of Nicaea.
After defeating this man at the Battle of Hydaspes, Alexander allowed him to continue ruling as a puppet king.

clues.clue: The campaign ended when his exhausted army mutinied and refused to cross the Beas (Hyphasis) River; his general Coenus urged the campaign's end and died shortly afterward
examples: Coenus urged its eventual termination and died shortly thereafter.
This campaign...ended after the invading army mutinied and refused to cross the Beas River.

clues.clue: Was shot in the lung by an arrow near the campaign's end while fighting an alliance of the Oxydracae and Malli; the costly return march home crossed the Gedrosian Desert
examples: Near the end of this campaign, its leader was shot in the lung with an arrow while fighting an alliance of the Oxydraci and the Malli.
The return from this campaign involved a costly march through the Gedrosian Desert.

works.name: Death of Alexander the Great
works.description: Alexander died in Babylon in June 323 BC at age 32 after a mysterious illness lasting several days. Ancient and modern sources disagree over whether he died of poison or disease, and his death immediately sparked the Wars of the Diadochi over succession.

clues.clue: Died in Babylon in June 323 BC at age 32 after a mysterious illness of several days, an event recorded in the diary of a Babylonian astronomer, who wrote 'the king died'
examples: It is known this event happened on June 10th or 11th, because a Babylonian astronomer recorded in his log that day that 'the king died.'
name this object that resulted from a probable case of typhoid in Babylon, leading to the death of a conqueror at age 32.

clues.clue: Shortly before his death, gave his signet ring to his general Perdiccas; his half-brother Arrhidaeus and his unborn son (by Roxane) were then made joint successors
examples: Shortly before this happened, the main person involved gave his signet ring to his general Perdiccas, and shortly after it, Arrhidaeus and a not-yet-born child were made dual monarchs.

clues.clue: A persistent legend held that the poison used to kill him could only be carried in the hollowed-out hoof of a mule/horse, since no other vessel could hold it; the gymnosophist Calanus reportedly predicted the death before self-immolating
examples: Another version centers on a hollowed-out mule's hoof.
The gymnosophist Kalanos allegedly predicted this event right before self-immolating.

clues.clue: Some apocryphal accounts blamed the regent Antipater or his son Cassander for the death, allegedly using poison supplied by Aristotle
examples: Apocryphal sources blame it on Cassander, who used poison provided by Aristotle.

clues.clue: Darius III's mother Sisygambis starved herself to death upon hearing news of Alexander's death; the death itself sparked the Wars of the Diadochi
examples: Sisygambis locked herself in her room and starved herself to death following this event.
name this event that occurred in Babylon in 323 BC, sparking the Wars of the Diadochi.

clues.clue: His body was later claimed to be preserved in honey inside a sarcophagus; a modern theory by researcher Katherine Hall proposed that the apparent lack of decomposition could be explained by a paralytic illness (linked to C. jejuni bacteria) rather than true death
examples: Katherine Hall theorized that Plutarch's account of this object's unusual longevity may be explained by C. jejuni bacteria.
After his death, Ptolemy I Soter took Alexander's body to Alexandria, where it was claimed to be entombed in this substance.

works.name: Wars of the Diadochi
works.description: Alexander's death without a clear adult heir triggered decades of warfare among his generals (the Diadochi, or 'successors'), who carved his empire into rival Hellenistic kingdoms and extinguished his own bloodline in the process.

clues.clue: While his body was being transported to Aegae for burial, his general Ptolemy I Soter seized the funeral cortege and diverted it to Egypt, triggering the First War of the Diadochi
examples: This ruler was not buried at Aigai because one of his generals, Ptolemy I Soter, seized his body while it was en route.
Ptolemy I triggered the first War of the Diadochi by stealing this object to bolster his legitimacy, forcing Perdiccas to attack Egypt.

clues.clue: His generals divided his empire at the Partition of Babylon, with Perdiccas serving as regent; these generals became known as the Diadochi ('successors')
examples: Name these generals who divided up the Hellenistic Empire at the Partition of Babylon.
Many generals of Alexander the Great are known by this title because they laid claim to various parts of his empire after his death and sought to succeed him.

clues.clue: His mother Olympias, wife Roxane, and young son Alexander IV were all eventually killed as Cassander usurped the Argead dynasty; Alexander IV was poisoned on Cassander's orders by Glaucias
examples: Glaucias's poisoning of this king and his mother Roxana ended the Argead Dynasty, allowing Cassander to assume the throne.

clues.clue: The Wars of the Diadochi culminated in the 301 BC Battle of Ipsus, where Antigonus the One-Eyed (who sought to reunite Alexander's empire) was killed
examples: Name this 301 BCE battle fought in Phrygia by members of the Diadochi. This battle stopped Antigonus from reuniting the lands of an earlier ruler.
Antigonus the One-Eyed was killed during this battle.

clues.clue: Ptolemy I and Seleucus I founded the Ptolemaic and Seleucid dynasties, respectively, out of his former empire
examples: During the Hellenistic age, Egypt was ruled by a dynasty founded by this leader, one of the Macedonian generals who had conquered the Persian Empire.
Both Antiochus III's Seleucid Empire and Ptolemaic Egypt were founded by diadochi...former military leaders who served this 'great' Macedonian king.

works.name: The Alexander Romance & Medieval Legend
works.description: A Greek-language 'Alexander Romance' attributed to Pseudo-Callisthenes became the wellspring for an enormous medieval legendary tradition depicting Alexander's fantastical exploits, spanning Christian European, Islamic, Persian, and West African sources.

clues.clue: The Greek-language 'Alexander Romance,' attributed to Pseudo-Callisthenes, became the source for a wide medieval genre depicting his legendary exploits across many traditions
examples: The most famous medieval work about this character was written by Pseudo-Callisthenes and names a genre of romances that depicted this character's life.
Name this protagonist of a Greek-language 'romance' that was popular throughout the pre-Islamic Arab world.

clues.clue: In legend, journeyed with his cook/servant Andreas to the Land of Darkness in search of the Water of Life (Fountain of Youth); Andreas accidentally used it while reviving a fish, but Alexander himself failed to attain immortality
examples: This man's cook Andreas revives a fish while cooking in the water of life, but this man fails to acquire immortality by drinking it.
In other stories, Alexander and his servant cross the Land of Darkness to reach this spring, whose restorative waters were sought by Juan Ponce de Leon in Florida.

clues.clue: Legendarily explored the top of the world carried aloft by griffins and the bottom of the ocean inside a glass submarine or diving bell
examples: Many works about this character depict his trips to the top of the world with the aid of griffins and to the bottom of the ocean in a glass submarine.
An early Mughal miniature depicts this historical figure being lowered into the sea in a glass bell.

clues.clue: Identified in the Quran's Sura al-Kahf with Dhu'l-Qarnayn ('the Two-Horned One'), who built a great wall/gate to imprison the destructive peoples Gog and Magog (Yajuj and Majuj) until Judgment Day
examples: This ruler legendarily built the Caspian Gates to keep out Gog and Magog, a tale related in the Quran, in which this ruler is referred to as Dhu al-Qarnayn.
The Gates of Alexander were supposedly built to keep out these peoples, who were sometimes identified with the Mongols or the Scythians.

clues.clue: In Ferdowsi's Persian epic the Shahnameh, is depicted as the half-brother of Darius through his mother Nahid, who was sent back to Persia after being rejected at court for her bad breath
examples: In one tradition, this character's mother Nahid is sent back to her homeland after being rejected at court for her bad breath.
This character is the half-brother of Darius in the Shahnamah.

clues.clue: In an Egyptian legend of his birth, is made the son of the exiled pharaoh Nectanebo II, who disguised himself as the god Ammon to secretly father him with Olympias
examples: This man is the son of Nectanebo, a man who disguises himself as Ammon to impregnate his mother.

clues.clue: West African oral epic tradition (the Epic of Sundiata) calls him 'a king of gold and silver' and compares the hero Sundiata to him; some traditions credit his descendants with founding the Ghana Empire's Cisse dynasty
examples: In the Epic of Sundiata, this ruler is referred to as 'a king of gold and silver' and Sundiata is most commonly compared to him.
The Epic of Sundiata also claims that descendants of this man founded the Cisse dynasty, which founded the Ghana Empire.

works.name: Alexander Mosaic
works.description: A Roman floor mosaic discovered in the House of the Faun at Pompeii, depicting Alexander in battle against Darius III and believed to be based on a lost Hellenistic painting.

clues.clue: Discovered on the floor of the House of the Faun at Pompeii, this Roman floor mosaic (using the opus vermiculatum technique) depicts Alexander battling Darius III, whose charioteer flees as a nobleman is impaled defending him
examples: name this work found on the floor of the House of the Faun in Pompeii, an arrangement of tiles depicting a Macedonian general.
This work depicts a nobleman impaled on the title figure's spear as he defends his king, who looks on helplessly with an outstretched arm as his charioteer wheels his horses away.

clues.clue: Alexander appears helmetless in the mosaic, his breastplate decorated with the head of Medusa; the work is thought to be based on a lost painting by Philoxenus of Eretria made for the Diadoch Cassander
examples: This man appears helmetless in a namesake mosaic in the House of the Faun in Pompeii.
The title figure's breastplate is decorated with the head of Medusa in this work, which was likely based on a painting described by Pliny the Elder, made for Cassander by Philoxenos of Eretrea.

clues.clue: The composition leaves only a single leafless tree visible against the crowded battlefield, with a dying soldier's face reflected in his own round shield
examples: The horizon is obscured by the mass of figures in this work, leaving a single leafless tree for the background.
In the center-right of this work, the face of a dying man in a yellow headscarf is reflected in his large circular shield.

works.name: The Battle of Issus (Albrecht Altdorfer painting)
works.description: German Danube School painter Albrecht Altdorfer's 1529 masterpiece depicts Alexander's victory over Darius III at the Battle of Issus, commissioned by Duke William IV of Bavaria and now displayed in Munich's Alte Pinakothek.

clues.clue: Commissioned by Duke William IV of Bavaria after the 1529 Siege of Vienna, this painting by Albrecht Altdorfer depicts Alexander's victory over Darius III at the Battle of Issus
examples: Duke William IV commissioned The Battle of Issus, which depicts this leader defeating the Persians led by Darius III.
William IV of Bavaria commissioned a painting celebrating this man after the 1529 Siege of Vienna.

clues.clue: Shows a giant Latin inscription on a tablet floating in the sky above the armies, with a crescent moon in the upper left (symbolizing Muslim defeat) and a sun in the upper right
examples: A more famous painting depicting this man shows a moon on the top left while a sun occupies the top right.
In that painting, a giant scroll in Latin flies over the top of the soldiers fighting on the ground.

clues.clue: Is an anachronistic 'world landscape' that unrealistically shows both Cyprus and the Nile River, with soldiers dressed in contemporary 16th-century armor rather than ancient dress
examples: Unrealistically, both Cyprus and the Nile River can be seen in this painting, which is the most famous to come out of the Danube School of 'world landscape' painters.
This painting shows a huge banner in the sky over participants in the title event, who wear anachronistic armor.

clues.clue: Is one of five paintings from the same cycle commissioned by William IV of Bavaria, all today displayed together in Munich's Alte Pinakothek
examples: This painting is, along with four other paintings from the same cycle commissioned by William IV of Bavaria, displayed in the Alte Pinakothek.

works.name: Apelles' Painting Campaspe in the Presence of Alexander
works.description: A recurring subject in later European painting depicting a legendary episode in which Alexander commissioned his court painter Apelles to paint his mistress Campaspe nude, then gave her to Apelles after the painter fell in love with her.

clues.clue: Legend holds Alexander commissioned the Greek painter Apelles to paint his mistress Campaspe nude, then gifted her to Apelles after the painter fell in love with her while painting; the scene was later depicted by artists including Jacques-Louis David and Giovanni Battista Tiepolo
examples: Jacques-Louis David depicted this man on the left of a painting that shows a naked woman sitting on a bed in Apelles Painting Campaspe in the Presence of [This Figure].
In one work, Giovanni Tiepolo painted this man and Campaspe in the studio of Apelles.

clues.clue: In one version of this scene, the Farnese Hercules statue appears in the background while an African man watches the artist paint
examples: The Farnese Hercules can be seen in the background of a Tiepolo painting where an African man stares at the artist painting his mistress' portrait.

works.name: Alexander Sarcophagus
works.description: An elaborately carved Hellenistic sarcophagus discovered at the Royal Necropolis in Sidon; despite its popular name and its depiction of Alexander in battle, it did not actually hold his body.

clues.clue: This temple-shaped, elaborately carved sarcophagus depicts Alexander hunting lions and was discovered in the Royal Necropolis at Sidon, where it likely held the remains of the local king Abdalonymos rather than Alexander himself
examples: That temple-shaped sarcophagus containing King Abdalonymos depicts this figure hunting lions with a gardener and was discovered at the Royal Necropolis in Sidon.
Despite its name, a sarcophagus that depicts the Battle of Issus does not contain this object.

clues.clue: Features thin-necked lions carved at each corner of its pediment and an acroter decorated with woman-headed eagle figures
examples: One work named for this figure includes thin-necked lions at the corner of each pediment and an acroter decorated with woman-headed eagles.

works.name: Legacy: Modern Memory
works.description: Alexander's legacy persists in modern political symbolism, notably a decades-long diplomatic dispute between Greece and the country now called North Macedonia over rights to his legacy and name.

clues.clue: A modern dispute between Greece and the Republic of (North) Macedonia over rights to Alexander's legacy involved the Vergina Sun symbol, the 1995 Interim Accord, and the U.N. acronym 'F.Y.R.O.M.'
examples: An agreement in this conflict, the 1995 Interim Accord, forced a participant to replace a flag that shows the Vergina Sun.
To avoid inflaming this conflict, the U.N. uses the acronym F.Y.R.O.M. to refer to one of the countries involved.

clues.clue: Oliver Stone shot his 2004 biopic Alexander in Thailand and Morocco rather than the Balkans partly to avoid inflaming this dispute; North Macedonia's 'antiquization' policy (statues like Warrior on a Horse) further escalated it
examples: To avoid escalating this disagreement, Oliver Stone shot a 2004 film in Thailand and Morocco.
This conflict was exacerbated by the VMRO's policy of antiquization, which included the construction of statues like Warrior on a Horse and the Founder of Heraclea in Bitola.

comprehensive_summary: Alexander the Great, the son of Philip II of Macedon and Olympias, was tutored by Aristotle (who agreed to the arrangement only after Philip rebuilt Aristotle's hometown Stagira) and mainly studied with him at Mieza alongside future successors like Cassander and Ptolemy. After inheriting the Macedonian throne, Alexander invaded the Achaemenid Persian Empire of Darius III, winning his first major victory at the Granicus over satraps commanded by Memnon of Rhodes (where his officer Cleitus the Black saved his life, an act Alexander later repaid by killing Cleitus in a drunken quarrel), then defeated Darius directly at Issus, besieged the island city of Tyre by building a causeway that permanently turned it into a peninsula, and won a final decisive victory at Gaugamela that effectively ended the Achaemenid Empire, prompting the satrap Bessus to kill Darius. At the Siwa Oasis oracle of Zeus-Ammon in Egypt, Alexander was told he was the son of the god Ammon, a claim to divinity supported by legends about his mother Olympias's dreams and later depicted by his conflation with the horned Quranic figure Dhu'l-Qarnayn. He pushed his conquests into India, besieging the 'birdless rock' Aornos and defeating King Porus at the Battle of the Hydaspes (where his horse Bucephalus died), before his exhausted army mutinied at the Beas River and forced him to turn back through the Gedrosian Desert. He died in Babylon in June 323 BC at age 32 after a mysterious illness, an event recorded by a Babylonian astronomer and long debated by historians as either poison (via a mule's-hoof-borne toxin, per persistent legend) or disease; his death triggered the Wars of the Diadochi, as his generals Ptolemy I, Seleucus I, Antigonus the One-Eyed, and others carved up his empire, extinguishing his Argead bloodline (his son Alexander IV was later poisoned on Cassander's orders) and fighting decades of wars that culminated in the 301 BC Battle of Ipsus. Alexander's afterlife in legend was even more expansive than his conquests: the Greek Alexander Romance attributed to Pseudo-Callisthenes spawned a vast medieval literary tradition in which he journeyed to the Land of Darkness seeking the Water of Life, explored the world's heights via griffins and its ocean depths in a glass submarine, and appeared across Persian (Shahnameh), Islamic (Quranic Dhu'l-Qarnayn), and West African (Epic of Sundiata) traditions. His image and battles became recurring subjects of later European art, including the Pompeii Alexander Mosaic, Albrecht Altdorfer's 1529 painting The Battle of Issus, depictions of Apelles Painting Campaspe in the Presence of Alexander, and the misleadingly named Alexander Sarcophagus, while his legacy persists into modern politics through the Greek-Macedonian naming dispute.
recursive_suggestions: Wars of the Diadochi — rich standalone content on the successor wars (Battle of Ipsus, Battle of Corupedium, Partition of Babylon) beyond what fits on Alexander's own page
Ptolemy I Soter — recurring Diadoch with independent detail (founding of Ptolemaic Egypt, seizure of Alexander's body, patron of Lysippos)
Seleucus I Nicator — recurring Diadoch, founder of the Seleucid Empire, war elephants from Chandragupta Maurya
Darius III — the Achaemenid king Alexander defeated at Issus and Gaugamela; appeared as part of a paired answerline ("Alexander the Great and Darius III")
Battle of the Hydaspes / King Porus — appeared as its own answerline; detailed content on the elephant tactics and aftermath
The Alexander Romance — appeared as extensive cross-cultural legendary material (Persian, Islamic, West African); could support its own Literature/Mythology-crossover page

Cilician Gates — appeared as its own answerline but content was mostly about later Roman civil war battles (Severus vs. Niger, Licinius vs. Maximinus Daza) fought at that location, not about Alexander himself; excluded here as off-topic
Philip II of Macedon — Alexander's father, likely warrants (or already has) an independent page
